w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Gemini Help with Architecture for Simple Planner App
Can you generate an architectural diagram image
for a very simple personal planner
? The original concept was a smart excel sheet that updated automatically once
, as can be seen in the attached image – a screenshot of an Instagram post displaying someone using something like this.

The app would be hosted on Azure Cloud infrastructure. My assumption is that a static hosted website from a storage account with simple html would work for this, but I do not have the experience to know if that’s true.
It is very important that the app look both aesthetic and well organized, with customizable colour-coding for various UI elements.
Unlike the image in the attachment, the various elements can be displayed in different viewing panes or internal tabs.
Key requirements (most reference features visible in the planner shown in the attached image):
The app has multiple viewing panes
: a weekly habits view, a weekly to do list view and a daily to do lists view for the selected week
Selectable weeks from a calendar view to be able to see and edit data for past, present and future weeks
Weekly habits checklist for each day like “morning walk” and “take vitamins” with a simple checkbox next to them for each day of the week
A general weekly “to do” list with checkboxes next to the task name
To do lists under each day with checkboxes next to the task name
An events list for each day with a time next to the event name
A focus box at the top of each day’s area in the UI to present a daily focus
A progress bar (percentage or number out of total score) showing completed tasks against the whole planned for the day/week sitting above each to do list (including the daily ones and the weekly one)
to do tasks will roll over into the next day (or week if under the weekly list) if not checked as complete that day (so the app needs to be able to read the date and time)
The events will be highlighted as in the past, present or future based on the current time, with particular focus on the present event
The current week will be highlighted slightly differently to past and future weeks to clearly distinguish it, allowing the user to easily find it
Tasks can be added to the task lists (either daily or weekly) easily

All tasks are displayed in the weekly to do list, with daily to do lists populated based on task deadline and priority, with optional user override (like a pin icon that can be set to ensure the task is set on that day regardless of priority and deadline, i.e. artificially setting the deadline to “today” for pinned tasks and allowing them to always roll over to the current day until complete)
The user can easily switch between the weekly habits, weekly to do list and daily lists for this week views

This architectural diagram outlines the best approach for building your "Smart Planner" web application within the Azure ecosystem, taking your original "smart excel sheet" concept and evolving it into a robust, scalable cloud solution.
Why a Simple HTML Site in Azure Storage is Not Enough

While you can indeed host static HTML, CSS, and client-side JavaScript from a cost-effective Azure Storage Blob container (configured as a "static website"), this approach is insufficient for your core requirements. Logic like automatic task rollover (at midnight), dynamic task prioritization, and event highlighting requires an active application server to read dates and modify your database.

, which are serverless API end-points. The client-side application (running in the user's browser) calls these functions (e.g., when a user checks a task, or when the page loads to get the list) to interact with a centralized database (Azure Cosmos DB). Furthermore, a special "Timer Triggered" Azure Function runs automatically on a schedule (like every midnight) to handle the critical task rollover logic you requested. This keeps the logic independent of the user's browser state.
Securely manages user identities and authenticates users into the application, ensuring that only you (or authorized users) can access and edit your personal data.

Cost-effectively hosts the actual files (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) that the user downloads. Azure CDN (Content Delivery Network) can be added to deliver these files even faster to users in different geographic locations.
These are stateless API end-points that your application calls to perform CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations on your planner data.
Crucial for your automated logic. It runs automatically every day (e.g., midnight) to find incomplete tasks in the database and roll their "due date" forward to the next day.
A flexible, fast NoSQL database that efficiently stores diverse data structures like user profiles, weekly task lists, habit tracking grids, and daily timelines.
The mockup demonstrates how the application meets all of your feature requirements while adhering to an "aesthetic and well-organized" aesthetic.
The palette at the bottom allows you to set custom color schemes for high/medium/low priority tasks, categories, and background elements, personalized exactly to your taste.
Allows easy switching between the 'Dashboard', 'Weekly View', 'Daily View', and 'Habits Tracker'.
Visible at the top, allowing you to easily view and edit data for any past, present, or future week.
Displays your comprehensive "Weekly To-Do List" (with deadlines, priorities, and a "Weekly Tasks Complete" progress bar) alongside your "Weekly Habits Checklist" grid.
Features the "Daily Focus" box, an "Events Timeline" that clearly highlights the present event, and your specific "Daily To-Do List" (with priority labels and a "Monday Tasks Complete" progress bar).

Call-out graphics demonstrate how incomplete tasks (like the ones from "Monday") are automatically rolled over to "Tuesday." You can also see the "pin" icon in the Daily To-Do list, enabling your "user override" feature to artificially pin a task to a specific day regardless of its original deadline or priority.
This design provides a clear path to building a powerful, customized personal planner that automates the mundane parts of organization, just like the smart excel sheet that first inspired you.

w:r.w:t: Is Azure Storage Static Website the best tool to use here? What would the benefits of a fully coded REACT web application be over this in the context of providing a simple formatted planner with checkboxes, buttons (like the "pin" feature or filtering options) and text inputs?
To answer your first question directly:
is a solid, cost-effective choice for hosting, but for this specific "Smart Planner" project, it’s likely not the
. While it sounds similar, SWA is designed specifically for modern frameworks like React. It bundles your front-end hosting with your
(Azure Functions) and your database connections into one seamless workflow.
Here is the breakdown of why moving from a "simple HTML" approach to a
is the right move for your vision.
In a simple HTML/JS site, if you check a box, you
manually tell the code to find that specific element in the document, change its style, and then send a message to the database. It gets messy fast.
React uses "State." If you click a "Pin" button, you just update a single piece of data (
: true). React automatically detects that change and
only the parts of the UI that need to change. This is what makes the app feel "snappy" and professional rather than clunky.
Your planner has many repeating elements: checkboxes, task rows, and progress bars.
You’d be copy-pasting code for every single task row, making it a nightmare to change the design later.
/> component. If you decide to change the "checked"
from green to blue, you change it in
place, and it updates across the Daily, Weekly, and Habit views instantly.
You mentioned progress bars that update automatically and filters for priority.
. You don't write code to "move the progress bar." Instead, you write code that says:
"The progress bar is always equal to (Completed Tasks / Total Tasks)."
As soon as a checkbox is clicked, the bar moves automatically because the underlying data changed.
Your requirement for tasks to "roll over" or be "pinned" requires a bit of logic.
React can easily "talk" to your Azure Functions. When the app loads, React can run a quick check:
"Is the current date later than the task date?"

and instantly sort those tasks into the current day’s view. Doing this with "simple HTML" requires much more complex manual "DOM manipulation" (the technical term for manually poking the webpage to change things).
Easy to integrate UI libraries (like Tailwind).
If you want this to feel like a "product" rather than a "project,"
React is the way to go.
It will make the "Smart" part of your "Smart Excel" concept much easier to build and maintain.

w:r.w:t: What networking, monitoring and security resources should I consider
this up in a secure, controllable, easily monitored environment? For example: which infrastructure resources should be contained within a
; which ones should have private endpoints; what supporting resources (NSGs, WAFs, etc) are needed to secure the resources in the
(s); what azure monitoring resources would provide security and/or performance insights over the infrastructure and in what formats; etc
Transitioning from a basic web setup to a "hardened" environment means moving away from the public internet wherever possible. Since you’ve opted for the
front-end, your security strategy shifts toward protecting the "connective tissue" between your UI, your logic (Functions), and your data (Cosmos DB).
Here is the blueprint for a secure, controllable, and observable Azure environment.
Even though SWA is a managed service, you can "isolate" your backend
so they never touch the public internet.
For your Cosmos DB Private Endpoint.
This is the "gold standard" for security. You should disable public access to your
and instead give it a Private Endpoint within your
. This gives the database a private IP address that is only reachable from your Azure Functions.
Configure your Azure Functions to use "Outbound
Integration." This allows the Function code to "reach into" the
to talk to the Database securely.
To protect against external threats and control internal traffic, you'll need these "gatekeeper" resources:
Azure Front Door + WAF (Web Application Firewall):
Front Door acts as a global entry point. The WAF sits on top of it to block common attacks like SQL Injection or Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) before they ever reach your React app.
accept traffic coming from your specific Front Door instance.
* Think of these as internal firewalls for your subnets.
You would apply an NSG to the Data Subnet that says:
"Only allow incoming traffic from the Backend Subnet on port 443; deny everything else."
* Never hardcode your database connection strings or API keys in your React code or Function settings. Store them in Key Vault and have your Functions "fetch" them at runtime using a
To ensure the app is healthy and to catch security anomalies, you should centralize your logs.
All logs from every resource (SWA, Functions, Cosmos DB,
) should be sent here. It acts as the "brain" for your monitoring.
Did a user's "Pin Task" button fail? You'll see the exact line of code that crashed.
See which "viewing panes" are most popular.
You can create a single "Widescreen" dashboard that displays:
This provides a "Secure Score." It will proactively tell you if you forgot to turn on a firewall or if a resource is exposed to the internet.
Keeps data traffic off the public internet.
Blocks bots and web attacks at the edge.
Allows Functions to talk to DB without passwords.
Secure storage for API keys and strings.
Building this out can feel like a lot for a personal project, but starting with the
gives you the most "bang for your buck" in terms of control.
"Would you like me to provide a specific "Budget vs. Enterprise" breakdown of what these resources might cost you monthly?"
Yes please. Initially, I intend to have just a handful of test users including myself, so minimal costs are preferred.

For a project starting with just you and a few test users, the price difference between a "Budget" and "Enterprise" setup is significant. Azure is uniquely generous with free tiers for developers, but "locking down" the environment with
and WAFs triggers base hourly charges that apply regardless of how little traffic you have.
Here is the 2026 cost breakdown for your Smart Planner.
Goal: Maximum functionality for the price of a cup of coffee.
In this tier, we rely on
, you use "Access Restrictions" to ensure your Functions only talk to your Database, and your Database only talks to your Functions.
, you can secure this by setting a "Firewall Rule" on Cosmos DB to only allow the specific Outbound IP addresses of your Azure Functions.
Goal: Zero public footprint and proactive threat protection.
. These resources have "provisioning costs"—flat hourly rates you pay just for them being "turned on."
is practically free because you are using shared infrastructure. Your data is still encrypted, but your database has a "public" endpoint (even if it's locked behind an IP firewall).
even if zero people use the app. This is the "tax" for having a private, isolated network where your database has no public IP address at all.
Since you are in the "handful of users" phase,
Azure makes it very easy to "upgrade" later. You can build your React app on the Free tier of Static Web Apps today. Once you have 100+ users or sensitive data that legally requires a
, you can flip the switch to Standard, provision your Private Endpoints, and the code remains
Would you like me to generate a "Budget-Friendly" Security Checklist to help you lock down the free tier without spending that $90/month?
budget, we can’t use expensive "physical" walls like
or Private Endpoints. Instead, we will use
This "Budget-Friendly" checklist allows you to lock down the Free Tier so that only
can see your data, and your services only talk to each other—without the $90/month overhead.
On the Free Tier, your app is technically public. To make it private:
provider to only allow sign-ins from your specific "Tenant ID." This ensures that even if someone finds your URL, they cannot log in with a random Gmail or Outlook account.
Add a configuration file to your React project that blocks the entire site unless the user has the authenticated role.
This is the most professional way to secure a budget app.
. It essentially gives your code its own "ID badge."
, assign the "Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor" role directly to your Function's Identity.
. Your code doesn't contain a password; it simply asks Azure, "I am Function X, let me into Database Y," and Azure validates the identity internally.
, your Cosmos DB is technically on the public internet. Let's minimize that:
This is a "semi-private" wall—it blocks the general internet but allows other Azure services (like your Functions) to get through.
Add your home/office IP address to the allow-list so you can still use the "Data Explorer" in the Azure Portal to manually edit your tasks.
SWA automatically handles the security between the React front-end and the Functions. You should ensure your Functions are set to
auth level because SWA acts as a gateway, only passing through traffic from authenticated users of your site.
, set a data volume cap (e.g., 100MB/day). This prevents a bug or a bot from sending millions of logs and accidentally costing you money.
Turn on "Failure Anomalies." If your "Task Rollover" function fails three times in a row, Azure will send you a free email
so you don't wake up to an unorganized planner.
Restrict login to your Entra ID Tenant.
Set Cosmos DB to "Selected Networks" + "Allow Azure."
Set a Daily Data Cap on logs.
